title: Samsung revives 1.4nm foundry development to challenge TSMC and Intel, aims for mass production in 2029
gist: Samsung has restarted its 1.4nm (SF1.4) process development with mass production pushed from 2027 to 2029, engaging Applied Materials and Lam Research early and deploying ASML High-NA EUV at NRD-K; it trails Intel (2027) and TSMC (2028) but holds a High-NA edge over TSMC.

insights.claim: Samsung has restarted development of its 1.4nm process (codenamed SF1.4) with mass production pushed back to 2029 from an originally planned 2027, per The Bell.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: A two-year slip resets expectations for when Samsung can compete at the leading edge and lengthens the window its rivals hold node leadership.
insights.locator: Samsung is now restarting the development of its 1.4nm process technology

insights.claim: Samsung delayed 1.4nm specifically to strengthen its 2nm processes (SF2 and SF2P), having achieved high 2nm yields and won orders for Tesla's next-generation AI chips before advancing to 1.4nm.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: The Tesla AI-chip win and 2nm yield success validate Samsung's yield-first pivot and are the near-term foundry catalyst to track over the distant 1.4nm date.
insights.locator: Samsung's 1.4nm tech was originally planned to enter mass production next year

insights.claim: Samsung has asked equipment suppliers Applied Materials and Lam Research to begin early development of tools for the SF1.4 process, with the first tools going to its NRD-K R&D center.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Early tool-development requests are a leading indicator of real 1.4nm intent and near-term order flow for AMAT and Lam.
insights.locator: Samsung is looking to narrow the gap with its rivals

insights.claim: ASML has already delivered next-generation High-NA EUV to Samsung's NRD-K, to be used on select layers starting with the 1.4nm process.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Physical High-NA delivery confirms Samsung's commitment and gives it a tooling differentiator versus TSMC at 1.4nm.
insights.locator: ASML has delivered its next-generation High NA Extreme Ultraviolet

insights.claim: Samsung has also requested equipment to develop its next-generation V12 NAND, expected to enter full-scale mass production around 2030.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Parallel V12 NAND tooling signals Samsung's memory-side capex intentions on a ~2030 horizon.
insights.locator: Samsung has also requested orders for equipment to develop its next-generation V12 NAND

themes.theme: Competitive positioning vs TSMC and Intel

insights.claim: TSMC's 1.4nm node (A14) and Intel's 1.4nm-class node (Intel 14A) are fundamentally different processes despite the shared 1.4nm label.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Node names are marketing, not equivalence; compare on actual density/power rather than the 1.4nm label when handicapping the race.
insights.locator: While both are designated as 1.4nm products

insights.claim: Intel targets 1.4nm mass production in 2027 and TSMC in 2028, putting Samsung roughly one to two years behind, though Samsung holds an edge because TSMC is not yet using High-NA EUV.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: The High-NA gap is Samsung's one credible catch-up lever; monitor whether TSMC's non-High-NA path holds up on cost and yield at 1.4nm.
insights.locator: Samsung's rivals, Intel and TSMC, are ahead in the 1.4nm race
